stem: A circuit is built with a `ParameterVector`. Select ALL true statements.
code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.circuit import ParameterVector

x = ParameterVector('x', 3)
qc = QuantumCircuit(3)
for i in range(3):
    qc.ry(x[i], i)

options.text: `qc.assign_parameters({x: [0.1, 0.2, 0.3]})` binds the entire vector in one call.
A `ParameterVector` can only be bound one element at a time, never as a whole.

explanation.correct: A `ParameterVector('x', 3)` creates 3 distinct parameters named `x[0..2]` (A, B). You can bind the whole vector at once by using it as a single dict key mapped to a list of values (C).

distractors.D: Each index is a distinct `Parameter`; `x[0]` and `x[1]` are different objects that bind to different values.
distractors.E: A `ParameterVector` can be bound in one call (`{x: [...]}`) OR element by element — not only element-by-element.

verification.proof_script:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.circuit import ParameterVector

x = ParameterVector('x', 3)
qc = QuantumCircuit(3)
for i in range(3):
    qc.ry(x[i], i)
names = [p.name for p in qc.parameters]
np_ = qc.num_parameters
whole = qc.assign_parameters({x: [0.1, 0.2, 0.3]})
whole_free = whole.num_parameters
same = x[0] is x[1]
claims = {
    "A": np_ == 3,
    "B": names == ["x[0]", "x[1]", "x[2]"],
    "C": whole_free == 0,
    "D": same,
    "E": False,
}
evidence = {
    "A": f"num_parameters={np_} -> {claims['A']}",
    "B": f"parameter names={names} -> {claims['B']}",
    "C": f"assign_parameters({{x:[...]}}) left {whole_free} free -> whole-vector bind works {claims['C']}",
    "D": f"x[0] is x[1] -> {same}",
    "E": f"whole-vector bind succeeded (free={whole_free}) so element-by-element is NOT the only way -> {claims['E']}",
}
proven = [k for k, v in claims.items() if v]
emit_verdict({"answer": proven, "evidence": evidence, "observed": {"names": names, "num_parameters": np_}})
